title: Sts. Theodore General & Stratelates, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Augentius
narrative:
title": "Sts. Theodore General & Stratelates, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Augentius

narrative": "title\": \"Sts. Theodore General & Stratelates, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Augentius

narrative\": \"St. Theodore the General, St. Theodore Stratelates, St. Alexander of Alexandria, and St. Augentius the Hermit

On the seventh day of Yekatit, two soldier-martyrs whose names and vocations are so intertwined that the tradition often commemorates them together are joined by an Alexandrian patriarch of towering theological significance and by a little-known hermit whose life of prayer sustained the very world that the soldiers and the bishop laboured to protect. The two Theodores — Theodore the General of Heraclea and Theodore Stratelates of Amasia — are both military commanders, both martyred under Licinius, and both venerated throughout the Oriental Christian world as exemplars of the union of martial virtue and Christian faith. Theodore the General is remembered particularly for his burning of the pagan temple at Amasea, an act of iconoclastic zeal that cost him his life; Theodore Stratelates is honoured for the patience and courage with which he endured the most elaborate torments without wavering. Together they form a diptych of the Christian soldier: zeal on one side, endurance on the other.

What is less often noticed in the commemoration of the two Theodores is the radical reversal of values their martyrdom enacted. These were men of the highest military rank, accustomed to commanding armies, to being obeyed instantly, to occupying the apex of the imperial social hierarchy. Their refusal to sacrifice to the imperial gods was not merely a religious act but a political one: it was the declaration that there exists an authority before which all earthly rank dissolves, and that the Christian soldier answers ultimately to a Commander whose orders cannot be countermanded by any emperor. The Ethiopian Church venerates the military martyrs not because it valorises violence but because it recognises in their witness the possibility of total obedience — the obedience that lays down the sword of earthly authority in order to take up the cross.

Saint Alexander of Alexandria, the predecessor of Athanasius in the Alexandrian patriarchate, is the great hidden architect of the Nicene settlement. It was Alexander who first recognised the danger of Arius's teaching, who called the local synod that condemned it, and who fought strenuously for the calling of the universal council that met at Nicaea in 325. He came to Nicaea bearing the full weight of the Alexandrian theological tradition, accompanied by his young deacon Athanasius who would carry the battle forward for decades after him. Alexander's achievement was to ensure that the Church asked precisely the right question — not merely whether Arius was wrong, but what precisely must be affirmed about the Son — and his pastoral sensitivity in framing that question shaped the creedal formula that still structures Christian prayer.

Abba Augentius is the most obscure figure of this assembly and the one whom the Deep Assembly most urgently commends to discovery. A hermit of Gaul or possibly of Asia Minor, he is known chiefly through the brevity of his entry in the Synaxarium, which records a life of extraordinary fasting and prayer and a death so quiet that it was noticed only by the animals of the surrounding forest who gathered at his cell. The tradition says that Augentius prayed daily for soldiers and rulers, for those who bore the burden of temporal power, asking God to give them the grace the two Theodores had been given — the freedom to obey God before Caesar. The soldier-martyrs, the theologian-bishop, and the forgotten hermit are thus engaged in the same work, each from his own station: the redemption of power by the witness of love. Amen.
